stories.headline: Governor Polis signs bill expanding who can seek firearms restriction orders
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis on Monday signed Senate Bill 004 into law, broadening Colorado's extreme risk protection order (ERPO) law to allow health care facilities, mental health centers, and educational institutions to petition a court to temporarily restrict a person's access to firearms. The original 2019 law permitted only family members and law enforcement to seek such orders, which bar individuals deemed an imminent risk to themselves or others from possessing or purchasing firearms for up to 364 days.

A 2023 expansion added individual health care providers, mental health professionals, educators, and district attorneys to the list of eligible petitioners. The latest bill passed the Senate 20-13 and the House 39-24, with all Republicans and a handful of Democrats voting against it.

Sponsors Sens. Tom Sullivan and Julie Gonzales and Reps. Jenny Willford and Meg Froelich said the measure would reduce gun violence. Opponents argued it infringes on Second Amendment rights and due process protections under the Fifth Amendment.

stories.headline: Colorado state budget bill introduced in House as lawmakers debate $46.8 billion spending plan
stories.summary:
The Joint Budget Committee formally introduced Colorado's $46.8 billion annual spending bill in the House on Monday, setting up a week of floor debate and amendment battles. Of that total, roughly $17.3 billion is general fund money — up slightly from $17.1 billion in the current fiscal year — though lawmakers note that growth remains significantly below the rate of inflation.

The budget was crafted amid the state's latest deficit, driven largely by surging costs in Medicaid and the state prison system. Budget committee member Rep. Rick Taggart, R-Grand Junction, said the committee reduced spending in virtually every other department to compensate.

The House is expected to take up the bill on the floor Wednesday, where a marathon of proposed amendments is anticipated. If approved by the House by week's end, the bill will move to the Senate before heading to Gov. Jared Polis. Passing a balanced budget is a constitutional requirement for the legislature.

stories.headline: ICE arrested nearly 4,750 immigrants in Colorado during Trump's first year back in office
stories.summary:
Federal immigration authorities arrested 4,750 people without legal status in Colorado during the first year of President Donald Trump's second term, according to data analyzed by The Denver Post — nearly quadrupling the 1,202 arrests recorded in the preceding year. Of those arrested, 78% had a listed departure date, indicating they had already been removed from the United States.

The data, obtained from U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement by the Deportation Data Project, shows a dramatic shift in who is being targeted. During the pre-Trump year, more than 58% of Colorado arrestees had prior criminal convictions; under Trump, that share dropped to 35%, while those with no criminal history rose from 17.7% to 38%.

Arrestees came from more than 80 countries, with 2,001 from Mexico and 782 from Venezuela. At least 121 were younger than 18, and ages ranged from infants to a 91-year-old Mexican man. Five Venezuelans were removed under the Alien Enemies Act and transferred to a Texas facility before disappearing from the dataset.

stories.headline: Front Range passenger rail service officially named the Colorado Connector
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis and Front Range Passenger Rail District officials announced Monday that the planned passenger rail service linking Front Range cities will be called the Colorado Connector — or CoCo for short — following a public naming contest that drew more than 25,000 votes statewide. The name was chosen from four finalists: Colorado Connector, Colorado Range, Rangelink, and Front Range Express Destinations (FRED).

The announcement was made at Denver Union Station, the planned hub of a service that would eventually connect Fort Collins to Trinidad along the I-25 corridor, with a target launch of 2029. A "starter service" linking Denver, Boulder, Longmont, and Fort Collins would be funded through the Regional Transportation District and a state fee on rental car rentals.

A half-cent sales tax ballot measure to help fund the multi-billion-dollar project could appear before Front Range county voters in November, with a final decision on that measure expected as late as August. Public meetings are scheduled in Longmont on April 8, Boulder on April 15, and Westminster on April 29.

stories.headline: $350 million gondola transit network above downtown Denver proposed at public presentation
stories.summary:
A Denver businessman unveiled plans Tuesday evening for a $350 million automated gondola transit system that would carry passengers in ride-hailed cabins 30 feet above downtown streets. The presentation by gondola manufacturers Doppelmayr, Leitner-Poma, and Vuba was held at 101 W. Colfax and organized by Ryan Ross, director of New Downtown Denver.

The proposed network would feature three loops with 14 stops, connecting Denver Union Station with Civic Center, looping around Civic Center, and linking Union Station with the Burnham Yard site near the Broncos' preferred stadium location, the Auraria campus, Elitch Gardens, and Coors Field. Cabins would travel at 25 to 35 miles per hour and could be summoned via a smartphone app called Den-Vair.

The Denver Department of Transportation and Infrastructure has not studied the feasibility of a downtown-wide gondola system. City officials have previously explored a single-gondola concept linking Union Station with the Highland neighborhood across Interstate 25 and the South Platte River, but that proposal has not advanced.

stories.headline: Wildfire near El Paso County destroys two structures; arson suspected in separate nearby incident
stories.summary:
A Monday afternoon wildfire in El Paso County, west of Interstate 25 between Pueblo and Colorado Springs, burned roughly 50 to 75 acres and destroyed a house and an outbuilding before an evacuation order was lifted shortly after 6 p.m. The blaze, called the Outback View fire, was declared human-caused by the Bureau of Land Management, though no further details were provided on its origin.

The El Paso County Sheriff's Office said it is separately investigating a suspected arson from Sunday in the same general area, with a press release expected Tuesday. Sgt. Kurt Smith told The Denver Post there is currently no evidence linking the two incidents.

The Hanover Fire Protection District said crews remained on scene to locate and extinguish hot spots. Investigators are examining the burned house as a potential source of Monday's fire.

stories.headline: Denver police traffic enforcement surges 121% as city records record 93 traffic deaths in 2025
stories.summary:
Denver police officers issued nearly 14,000 traffic citations through mid-March 2026, a 121% increase over the same period last year, according to court records obtained by The Denver Gazette. The enforcement surge follows years of declining citations that began with the COVID-19 pandemic and persisted well after restrictions lifted, with Denver writing fewer than 33,000 tickets in 2025 — less than 40% of the roughly 82,000 it issued at its 2017 peak.

The renewed enforcement coincides with Denver recording a record 93 traffic fatalities in 2025, including 35 pedestrians. City officials have launched a Vision Zero initiative aimed at eliminating traffic deaths, which now includes fixed speed cameras being installed at select locations.

Traffic safety experts and city officials have linked the rise in deaths to behavioral changes that took hold during the pandemic, including higher rates of speeding, red-light running, and aggressive driving. Denver's post-pandemic enforcement gap allowed those behaviors to normalize, the data suggests.

stories.headline: Aurora declares Stage I water shortage, restricts outdoor use immediately
stories.summary:
The Aurora City Council voted unanimously Monday night to declare a Stage I Water Shortage, placing immediate restrictions on outdoor water use across the city. The declaration takes effect right away and comes amid widespread drought conditions affecting the Front Range.

Details of the specific restrictions were not fully outlined in available reporting, but Stage I declarations typically limit outdoor watering schedules and prohibit certain non-essential water uses such as washing vehicles or filling pools. Aurora is one of the Denver metro area's largest water utilities, serving more than 400,000 residents.

The move follows Denver Water's previously reported Stage 1 drought restrictions, which are expected to cost the utility $30 to $70 million in revenue. Multiple Front Range water providers are taking steps to address declining supplies as the region grapples with record-low snowpack.

stories.headline: United Airlines raises baggage fees, restructures premium club pricing at Denver hub
stories.summary:
United Airlines announced changes to its baggage fee structure and tiered pricing for its premium club membership, with the new baggage fees taking effect for tickets purchased starting Friday, according to the Denver Business Journal. The airline cited higher fuel costs driven by the ongoing Iran conflict as a factor in the adjustments.

United Airlines is headquartered in Chicago but operates one of its largest hubs at Denver International Airport, making fee changes directly relevant to the millions of Colorado passengers who fly the carrier each year. The specifics of the new fee tiers were not fully detailed in available reporting.

The move follows broader airline industry trends of unbundling fares and raising ancillary fees as carriers manage elevated operating costs. United's changes may prompt competitive responses from other carriers serving Denver.

stories.headline: Denver preparing for dueling 4/20 concerts: Mile High 420 Fest at Civic Center, Snoop Dogg at Red Rocks
stories.summary:
Denver will host two major rap concerts on April 20, setting up a competition for cannabis holiday revelers. The Mile High 420 Festival at Civic Center Park will feature Juicy J, Paul Wall, Mike Jones, That Mexican OT, and Trap Dickey, with general admission tickets priced at $30 and VIP at $185. Meanwhile, Red Rocks Amphitheatre's 420 on the Rocks will be headlined by Snoop Dogg and Ice Cube, with tickets starting at $199.

The timing of the two events offers flexibility for fans: Mile High 420 Fest is a traditional daytime party with Juicy J performing at 4:20 p.m., while 420 on the Rocks begins at 7 p.m. with doors at 5:30 p.m. The scheduling theoretically allows attendees to participate in both events.

Organizers of Mile High 420 Fest have not publicly explained how the festival will navigate ongoing construction at Civic Center Park, and have not responded to press inquiries on the matter. The festival began charging admission in 2025 after years as a free event.

stories.headline: Former Nuggets coach Michael Malone set to be hired by University of North Carolina
stories.summary:
Michael Malone, who led the Denver Nuggets to their first NBA championship in 2023, is returning to coaching after being hired by the University of North Carolina, according to multiple reports Monday. Malone was fired by the Nuggets earlier this season amid a disappointing start and front-office tensions.

The move marks a transition from the NBA to college basketball for Malone, whose familiarity with the transfer portal era of college sports is seen as an asset by analysts. North Carolina is one of the most storied programs in college basketball history.

Malone spent nine seasons as the Nuggets' head coach, compiling a winning record and guiding Nikola Jokic and the team through multiple deep playoff runs before the 2023 title. His departure from Denver this season was seen as a surprise given his championship pedigree.

stories.headline: No. 2 Denver Pioneers face No. 1 Michigan in NCAA lacrosse showdown Thursday
stories.summary:
The second-ranked University of Denver men's lacrosse team will face top-ranked Michigan in a high-profile matchup Thursday at 6:30 p.m. on ESPN2. The last time the two programs met was in 2022 in a similarly significant stage.

The game represents one of the marquee college lacrosse matchups of the early season and carries national title implications given both programs' rankings. Denver has been a perennial powerhouse in collegiate lacrosse.

Colorado sports fans are encouraged to tune in as the Pioneers look to knock off the nation's top-ranked team on a national broadcast.

weather.notable: About 10 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I am very small and I have no money, so you can imagine the kind of stress I am under.

topStory.headline: Trump Sets Tuesday Deadline for Iran Deal, Threatens to Destroy Power Plants and Bridges
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump issued an expanded ultimatum to Iran on Monday, threatening to destroy the country's power plants, bridges, and broader civilian infrastructure if Tehran does not reopen the Strait of Hormuz to shipping traffic by 8 p.m. EDT Tuesday. "The entire country can be taken out in one night, and that night might be tomorrow night," Trump said at a White House news conference. He indicated the deadline was final, saying he had already granted Iran sufficient extensions.

Iran rejected a 45-day ceasefire proposal and instead conveyed a 10-point counter-proposal through Pakistan — a key mediator — calling for a permanent end to the war with guarantees against future attacks. "We only accept an end of the war with guarantees that we won't be attacked again," said Mojtaba Ferdousi Pour, head of Iran's diplomatic mission in Cairo. Meanwhile, the United States and Israel carried out fresh strikes Monday, killing more than 25 people, including the Revolutionary Guard's intelligence chief, and hitting an information and communication technology building at Tehran's Sharif University of Technology. An Iranian missile strike on Haifa killed four people.

The confrontation is now in its sixth week. Trump also addressed the recovery of two U.S. airmen following the shoot-down of an American fighter jet over Iran. Both crew members were rescued in separate operations — the pilot within hours of the incident and the second airman on Sunday after being stranded in Iranian territory, in what Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, and Joint Chiefs Chairman Dan Caine described as among the most complex rescue missions in American history, involving more than 150 aircraft.

Trump further threatened legal action against a news outlet that reported the second airman was missing while the rescue operation was still ongoing, arguing the disclosure endangered national security. "We're going to go to the media company that released it, and we're going to say, 'National security. Give it up or go to jail,'" Trump said. Jameel Jaffer of the Knight First Amendment Institute at Columbia University called the threat an effort to intimidate the press, noting that news organizations have a First Amendment right to publish stories on matters of public importance. The White House confirmed an investigation is underway.

The U.S. threats to target power plants and bridges have drawn warnings from international observers about potential violations of the laws of war, which prohibit deliberate attacks on civilian infrastructure. With Iran's rejection of the ceasefire proposal and Tuesday's deadline approaching, the conflict appears poised to escalate further, with significant implications for global oil markets and regional stability given the Strait of Hormuz's role as a critical chokepoint for world energy supplies.
